content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5660              322-5901            322-6441           322-6444

June 13, 1980

Roger Martinez, Member
City Council of Santa Clara
City Hall
1500 Warburton Avenue
Santa Clara, CA 95050

Dear Mr. Martinez:

Thank you for your letter of May 23, 1980, requesting
advice concerning the disqualification provisions of the
Political Reform Act.

The facts, as you relate them, are as follows: You
have an ownership interest with your parents in a certain
parcel of property near the University of Santa Clara, in
an area of mixed commercial, industrial and residential use.
The parcel is approximately 7500 square feet and contains a
residential structure which is presently unoccupied. It was
formerly used as a residential care facility. Future use of
the property is likely to be commercial (office or related
use). Immediately adjacent to your property is a parcel of
land which has been the subject of two applications before
the City Planning Commission for variances, both of which
were granted. The first variance was sought to permit the
residential structure to be used for commercial purposes;
the second was sought in order to obtain a reduction in the
number of required on-site parking spaces from seven to zero.
On May 20, 1980, the minutes of the Planning Commission on
the issue of the variance for parking came before the City
Council, of which you are a member, for approval. Although
you originally recommended that the City Council review the
decision of the Planning Commission, when your participation
was challenged on the grounds of your interest, another
Council member moved to appeal the matter. You abstained
from participation in that decision. Your question now is
whether you can participate in the decision on the appeal.

Generally, Government Code Sections 87100-87103 require
that a public official disqualify himself from making or
participating in making a decision which it is reasonably
foreseeable will have a material financial effect upon his

Roger Martinez
June 13, 1980
Page Two


own financial interests. The Act defines an interest of
$1,000 or more in real property as among the kinds of financial interests which can give rise to a prohibited conflict
of interest. By regulation the Commission has established
guidelines for determining the quantum of effect that generally
will be considered material in the case of a $1,000 or more
interest in real property. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)
provides:

     (2) Whether, in the case of a direct or indirect
interest in real property of one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more held by a public official, the effect of the
decision will be to increase or decrease:

          (A) The income producing potential of the
property by the lesser of:

               1. One thousand dollars ($1,000) per
month; or

               2. Five percent per month if it is
fifty dollars ($50) or more per month; or

          (B) The fair market value of the property
by the lesser of:

               1. Ten thousand dollars ($10,000); or

               2. One half of one percent if it is
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     Assuming, therefore, that your interest in the property
is worth more than $1,000, you need only disqualify yourself
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision on the
variance would affect either the rental value or the fair
market value of the property in a material fashion as set
forth in the regulation.

     I understand from our conversation that you have been
advised by the staff of the Planning Commission that the
requested variance for parking will not affect either the
rental or the fair market value of your property. Therefore,
unless you have reason to believe that this advice is unsound,
it is reasonable for you to rely upon it in concluding that
the variance will not have a material financial effect upon
your interests, and you need not disqualify yourself.

Roger Martinez
June 13, 1980
Page Three


     I hope that this advice proves helpful.  If you have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to call me.

                                    / Sincerely,

                                    [signature]
                                    Thomas M. Sobel
                                    Chief
                                    Legal Division

TMS:cjb

THE CITY OF SANTA CLARA
CALIFORNIA

Office of City Council                                    CITY HALL
                                                         1500 WARBURTON AVE.
                                                         984-3250

May 23, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
Sacramento, California  95814

Gentlemen:

I hereby respectfully request written advice from the Fair Political
Practices Commission for guidance in my official activities based
on the following facts which I believe are material:

MATERIAL FACTS:

I currently have an ownership interest, together with my parents,
in a parcel of real property located at 1138 Sherman Street, in the
City of Santa Clara.  The property is in an older area of the city,
near the University of Santa Clara, having a mix of residential,
industrial and residential uses.  The parcel is approximately
7,500 square feet and contains a residential structure, currently
unoccupied.  I anticipate that, because of its proximity to
commercial and light industrial concerns, the future use of the
property will be for an office or related use.

A small parcel of land known as 3370 The Alameda, immediately
adjacent to my property to the northwest, has been the subject of
two recent applications heard before the City Planning Commission.
On February 27, 1980, the Planning Commission approved a variance
application, permitting the residential structure on the Alameda
property to be used for commercial purposes.  The variance was
necessary because of the substandard size of the lot.  On May 14,
1980, the Planning Commission approved another variance on the
same parcel permitting a reduction in the number of required on-site
parking spaces from 7 to 0, subject to several stand City conditions.

At the City Council meeting of May 20, 1980, the minutes of the
Planning Commission came before the City Council for approval.  At
that time I recommended that the City Council undertake an appeal
of the Planning Commission action of May 14 regarding the parking
spaces pursuant to procedures provided for in the City Zoning
Ordinance.  The basis for my objection to the variance was the
elimination of needed on-site parking in a heavily trafficked area.
Thereupon, the applicant objected to my participation in any
recommendation for appeal because of my ownership interest in the
Sherman Street property.  The motion to appeal the matter was then

FPPC                           -2-                    May 23, 1980


instituted by a fellow councilman and I abstained from participation
in the matter so that I might seek advise from this Commission.

I have enclosed herewith an aerial photograph showing the relationship of the two parcels to one another. Again, the use of the
Sherman Street parcel owned by myself and my parents is currently
in transition. Until March of this year, it was used as a
residential care facility and I anticipate its use for office
purposes in the future. Accordingly, it is not now productive
of income and its value as an office is unknown.

REQUESTED OPINION:

In accordance with Government Code § 83114(b), as implemented by
your Regulation 18329, I respectfully request that the Fair
Political Practices Commission provide written advice with respect
to my conflict of interest duties in connection with the City
Council consideration of the above referenced appeal of the action
of the Planning Commission. The hearing on said appeal is
currently scheduled for Tuesday, June 17, 1980. If there are
any additional facts or other material you might desire, please
contact me so I can make them available to you. I would also
appreciate your expeditious handling of this request so that I
will have the benefit of the decision for use at the scheduled
public hearing.

                                Very truly yours,


                                _______________________________
                                Roger Martinez
                                City Councilman

enclosure

FREMONT ST.

SHERMAN ST.

BENTON ST.

THE ALAMO DA

sections.question: Whether the requester, a Santa Clara City Councilman with an ownership interest in real property at 1188 Sherman Street, has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act with respect to City Council consideration of an appeal concerning variance applications for an adjacent parcel at 3370 The Alameda, and whether he must abstain from voting on the matter.
sections.conclusion: The Commission advises that the requester has a financial interest in the property at 1188 Sherman Street that could be materially affected in value by the City Council's decision on the appeal. Under Government Code § 87100, the requester must not vote or take any other action on the matter and should recuse himself from the proceeding. The potential impact on property value meets the threshold for a disqualifying conflict of interest (fair market value impact of $1,000 or more or 1% or more).
sections.question_synthetic: Does a city councilman have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when his adjacent property could potentially be affected in value by the City Council's decision on variance applications for neighboring property, and must he abstain from voting?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the requester has a material financial interest in his property that could be affected by the Council's decision on the adjacent property variances, requiring him to abstain from voting and all participation in the matter under Government Code § 87100. The potential impact on property value exceeds the $1,000 threshold for a disqualifying conflict of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is fragmented with some text corruption/OCR errors making certain passages difficult to read with complete clarity. However, the core Q/A structure is discernible. The incoming request from the councilman (dated May 23, 1980) is followed by the FPPC's response letter (dated June 13, 1980). Confidence reduced due to text quality issues but main legal content is extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the requester, a Santa Clara City Councilman with an ownership interest in real property at 1188 Sherman Street, has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act with respect to City Council consideration of an appeal concerning variance applications for an adjacent parcel at 3370 The Alameda, and whether he must abstain from voting on the matter.

CONCLUSION: The Commission advises that the requester has a financial interest in the property at 1188 Sherman Street that could be materially affected in value by the City Council's decision on the appeal. Under Government Code § 87100, the requester must not vote or take any other action on the matter and should recuse himself from the proceeding. The potential impact on property value meets the threshold for a disqualifying conflict of interest (fair market value impact of $1,000 or more or 1% or more).

embedding.summary: A Santa Clara City Councilman sought FPPC advice regarding whether he has a conflict of interest in voting on a City Council appeal concerning variance applications for property adjacent to his own. The FPPC determined he must recuse himself due to a material financial interest in his property that could be affected by the decision.
